title: Intro to R & DataFest Prep

Workshop materials helping students prepare for DataFest through R collaboration, exploratory analysis, and presentation strategy.

View Slides

title: Intro to R & DataFest Prep
text:
Executive Summary
This workshop was designed to help students prepare for DataFest by strengthening both their technical workflow and their strategic approach to collaborative data analysis. The session covered practical guidance for working in R, collaborating with GitHub, beginning exploratory data analysis, and turning early findings into a focused presentation.
The workshop emphasized that successful data work is not only about finding the most complex model. It also requires scoping a question, understanding the project domain, identifying useful variables, creating clear graphics, and communicating a small number of meaningful takeaways to stakeholders.

title: Intro to R & DataFest Prep
text:
Project Highlights

Co-developed workshop materials introducing students to collaborative R workflows, exploratory analysis, and DataFest strategy.
Helped students think through GitHub practices, including shared file paths, .gitignore, modular scripts, and avoiding merge conflicts.
Taught presentation principles focused on curating content, highlighting key findings, and connecting results to stakeholder impact.

title: Intro to R & DataFest Prep
text:
Methods & Approach
The workshop was structured around four practical themes: collaboration, exploration, presentation, and general DataFest strategy. Students were encouraged to follow along in RStudio or Posit Cloud using supporting workshop materials.
The technical guidance focused on reproducible and collaborative workflows, including how to organize scripts, manage large data files, and work as a team in GitHub. The analytic guidance focused on exploratory data analysis: learning the project domain, using documentation, identifying missing data and outliers, creating simple graphics, narrowing scope, engineering features, and incorporating external data when useful.
The presentation guidance encouraged students to prioritize clarity over comprehensiveness. Instead of presenting every step of their analysis, students were advised to focus on one or two compelling ideas, use readable graphics, and connect findings to the needs of real stakeholders.

title: Intro to R & DataFest Prep
text:
Deliverables

Workshop Slides
A slide deck covering collaborative R workflows, exploratory data analysis, presentation strategy, and practical tips for students preparing for DataFest.
View Slides

A collaborative scaffolding tool that helps statisticians and domain experts translate evidence into action.

Visit Thesis Website Watch Poster Presentation

title: Designed For Impact
text:
A collaborative scaffolding tool that helps statisticians and domain experts translate evidence into action.

Visit Thesis Website Watch Poster Presentation

title: Designed For Impact
text:
Executive Summary
Designed For Impact: The Statistical Collaborator’s Workflow is my master’s thesis project focused on improving how statisticians and domain experts plan for real-world impact in collaborative projects. The project introduces the Designed for Impact Tool, a fillable framework that helps teams align on goals, define a decision-focused intended impact, connect analysis choices to that impact, and communicate results in ways that support action.
The work is motivated by a shift in the role of statisticians and data scientists. As technical analysis becomes increasingly automated, the value of a statistical collaborator depends not only on modeling and interpretation, but also on asking better questions, guiding stakeholder decisions, and helping teams translate evidence into action.

title: Designed For Impact
text:
Project Highlights

Developed a practical consulting tool that structures early project conversations around vision, partnership, impact, analysis pathway, and narrative.
Extended existing statistical collaboration frameworks by adding a more explicit structure for planning and assessing impact.
Coordinated a full research cycle, including framework development, instrument design, research administration, stakeholder-informed iteration, and thesis writing.

title: Designed For Impact
text:
Methods & Approach
The Designed for Impact Tool builds on established statistical collaboration frameworks, including QQQ and ASCCR, while addressing a gap in how statistical teams plan for impact. Rather than treating impact as something evaluated after analysis is complete, the tool helps collaborators define intended impact at the start of a project and use that definition to guide methodological and communication decisions.
The project combines framework development, applied research design, and mixed methods validation. I developed the tool through an iterative process by soliciting feedback from students and practitioners in computer-aided personal interviews.

title: Designed For Impact
text:
Deliverables

Thesis Website
A public-facing website introducing the Designed for Impact Tool, the motivation behind the project, and the research process supporting the framework.
Visit Website


Poster Presentation
A recorded eCOTS poster presentation summarizing the motivation, framework, and early research direction for the project.
Watch Presentation

View Manuscript

title: Quantitative Social Pressure Theory
text:
Collaborative research manuscript using large-scale survey data, statistical modeling, and visualization to evaluate a formal social pressure theory.

View Manuscript

title: Quantitative Social Pressure Theory
text:
Executive Summary
This collaborative research project evaluates a formal social pressure theory using large-scale questionnaire data from more than 570,000 respondents across 88 countries. The manuscript examines whether country-level measures of heteronormative social pressure are associated with differences in the distribution of sexual orientation scores across international samples.
My primary contributions focused on the statistical and visual components of the paper. I supported the analysis workflow, created many of the figures used to communicate model results, and contributed to manuscript preparation. The theoretical and mathematical foundations were developed collaboratively by the broader research team.

title: Quantitative Social Pressure Theory
text:
Project Highlights

Conducted statistical analyses for a large international dataset containing questionnaire responses from 572,128 people across 88 countries.
Created data visualizations to communicate distributional patterns, country-level comparisons, and relationships between social pressure measures and survey outcomes.
Contributed to manuscript development by supporting analysis interpretation, figure preparation, and written review.

title: Quantitative Social Pressure Theory
text:
Methods & Approach
The project used translated versions of an online questionnaire to study sexual orientation scores across international samples. The research team developed a country-level social pressure measure using policy and social acceptance variables, then compared those scores against country-level patterns in mean sexual orientation.
My work centered on translating the research questions into statistical summaries and visual evidence. This included analyzing distributions, comparing country-level patterns, producing publication-ready graphics, and helping communicate findings in a clear manuscript format. The analysis included distributional visualization, regression modeling, group comparisons, and graphical summaries of large-scale survey data.

title: Quantitative Social Pressure Theory
text:
Deliverables

Research Manuscript
A collaborative manuscript submitted for publication. The paper presents statistical and visual evidence from a large international survey dataset to evaluate a formal social pressure theory.
View Manuscript

title: Data Structures Repository

This archive complements my statistics and data science work by documenting a stronger foundation in computational thinking, program structure, and algorithmic problem-solving.

title: Telecommunications Churn Strategy
text:
Poster project using classification models to identify churn risk and translate model results into retention strategy.

View Poster View Repository

title: Telecommunications Churn Strategy
text:
Poster project using classification models to identify churn risk and translate model results into retention strategy.

View Poster View Repository

title: Telecommunications Churn Strategy
text:
Executive Summary
This project analyzed customer service records from a fictitious telecommunications company to identify which customer profiles were most strongly associated with churn. Using account, billing, and service subscription variables, I compared classification models and used the stronger model to estimate churn risk, identify key predictors, and translate model output into a targeted retention strategy.
The project connects predictive modeling with business decision-making. Rather than stopping at model performance, the analysis considered how churn probabilities, customer value, and outreach costs could be used together to prioritize retention resources.

title: Telecommunications Churn Strategy
text:
Project Highlights

Compared logistic regression and decision tree models using cross-validation, with logistic regression achieving stronger predictive performance.
Identified customer characteristics associated with higher churn risk, including month-to-month contracts, electronic check payments, no tech support plan, and higher total charges.
Developed a retention strategy framework by combining predicted churn probability, customer billing value, outreach cost, and expected reduction in churn risk.

title: Telecommunications Churn Strategy
text:
Methods & Approach
The analysis used customer account, billing, and service subscription variables to predict churn. Because the outcome was moderately imbalanced, I evaluated model performance using ROC-AUC and compared logistic regression against a decision tree approach. Logistic regression performed slightly better and was used to interpret the strongest predictors of churn.
After estimating churn probabilities, I translated the classification results into a practical retention strategy. Customers were segmented by both predicted churn risk and billing value to identify high-risk, high-value accounts. I then considered a simple expected-value scenario in which outreach costs $50 per customer and reduces churn probability by 20%, allowing the model results to inform which customers would be most worthwhile to target.

title: Telecommunications Churn Strategy
text:
Deliverables

Research Poster
A poster presentation summarizing the churn modeling process, key predictors of customer attrition, risk classification strategy, and business implications for targeted retention outreach.
View Poster


GitHub Repository
Source code for the churn analysis, including model fitting, validation, visualization, and retention strategy calculations.
View Repository

title: Nonprofit M&A

Leadership project developing governance recommendations for an eight-figure nonprofit integration in California higher education.

View Closeout Memo Request Supporting Materials

title: Nonprofit M&A
text:
Executive Summary
Associated Students, Inc. (ASI) is Cal Poly’s student-governed nonprofit auxiliary, responsible for representing students and supporting campus programs, services, and student leadership. During Cal Poly’s historic integration with Cal Maritime, ASI needed to develop a unified student governance structure that could incorporate Maritime students into one organization while preserving representation, continuity, and operational clarity.
As Chair of the ASI Cal Maritime Integration Ad Hoc Committee, I led a joint student governance process that brought together representatives from both campuses. Over the course of one year, the committee met 25 times, reported to multiple stakeholder groups, and produced three core recommendations: a shared governance model, responsibility guides for new Maritime student leadership positions, and a package of ASI Bylaws amendments to operationalize the integration.

title: Nonprofit M&A
text:
Project Highlights

Chaired a joint ad hoc committee with student representation from both ASI and the Associated Students of the California Maritime Academy.
Led the development of governance recommendations that established a new Maritime student council and formalized representation across ASI leadership structures.
Guided the committee through rescoping, cross-campus negotiation, policy review, and final recommendations approved by the ASI Board of Directors.

title: Nonprofit M&A
text:
Methods & Approach
This project required translating a large institutional integration into concrete governance decisions for a student-governed nonprofit. The committee’s work began with relationship-building, campus learning, and shared problem definition before moving into governance design, role development, and policy implementation.
A key challenge was that the legal and operational pathway changed during the project. The committee adapted from an initial merger-oriented plan toward a structure that supported ASCMA dissolution and integration into ASI. That shift required rescoping deliverables, maintaining stakeholder confidence, and ensuring that recommendations remained legally and operationally coherent.
The final recommendations included a shared governance framework, responsibility guides for new student leadership positions, and a bylaw amendment package that established the new governance bodies, clarified representation, and prepared ASI to welcome Maritime Academy students as members. The closeout memo describes these deliverables as the result of “early problem disaggregation, occasional rescoping, and measured execution.” :contentReferenceoaicite:0

title: Nonprofit M&A
text:
Deliverables

Project Closeout Memo
A public summary of the committee’s charge, scope changes, execution, implementation plan, and key deliverables from the integration process.
View Closeout Memo


Supporting Governance Materials
Additional materials are available upon request, including the Shared Governance Model, student leadership responsibility guides, and the ASI Bylaws amendment package used to operationalize the integration.
Request Supporting Materials

title: LIFT
text:

Launch App View Repository

title: LIFT
text:
Executive Summary
LIFT is a prototype AI-powered operations tool built around a fictitious nonprofit service scenario. The app demonstrates how organizations could use practical AI workflows to improve day-to-day operations, turn frontline notes into structured insights, and make more informed decisions about staffing, reporting, and resource allocation.
The project was designed as a public-facing demo rather than a live AI system. Instead of connecting to an external model, LIFT simulates an AI-assisted workflow through clickable interactions, guided outputs, and realistic nonprofit operations content. This makes the app easy to evaluate while still showing how thoughtful AI implementation could support mission-driven teams.

title: LIFT
text:
Project Highlights

Built an interactive Shiny application that demonstrates how AI could support nonprofit operations, service reporting, and decision-making.
Designed a guided user experience that translates messy operational information into structured summaries, insights, and recommended next steps.
Created a realistic demo environment to communicate AI implementation strategy without requiring live model calls or sensitive organizational data.

title: LIFT
text:
Methods & Approach
LIFT was developed as a Quarto/Shiny application with an emphasis on usability, storytelling, and applied AI strategy. The app is structured around a fictitious nonprofit case study, allowing users to move through a simulated workflow for reviewing operational information, identifying service challenges, and generating decision-ready outputs.
The goal was not simply to build a dashboard, but to prototype how AI could fit into an organization’s existing work. I focused on designing interactions that feel practical for nonprofit staff: clarifying problems, reducing reporting friction, surfacing patterns, and helping teams move from raw information to action.

title: LIFT
text:
Deliverables

Live Shiny App
A deployed prototype demonstrating an AI-assisted operations workflow for a fictitious nonprofit organization.
Launch App


GitHub Repository
Source code for the LIFT prototype, including the Quarto/Shiny app structure and supporting materials.
View Repository

title: Democracy Dashboard

Interactive dashboard applying survival analysis and geospatial visualization to explore democratic transitions and coup histories.

Launch Dashboard View Repository

title: Democracy Dashboard
text:
Executive Summary
The Democracy Dashboard is a collaborative Shiny project for exploring global histories of democratic and non-democratic regimes. The dashboard combines interactive maps, statistical summaries, and model interpretation to help users investigate how countries move between regime types and how long non-democratic spells last before returning to democracy.
The project uses democracy, dictatorship, and coup d’état datasets to make comparative political history more accessible. Users can explore global regime classifications, review country-level histories, examine regional governance patterns, and investigate successful and unsuccessful coup attempts by country.

title: Democracy Dashboard
text:
Project Highlights

Built an interactive dashboard that combines geospatial visualization, statistical modeling, and political regime data.
Applied a Cox proportional hazards model to analyze how regime characteristics are associated with return to democracy.
Designed user-facing explanations that translate technical modeling concepts, including hazard ratios, into accessible dashboard text.

title: Democracy Dashboard
text:
Methods & Approach
The dashboard was built with Shiny to support interactive exploration of global governance data. One section visualizes democratic and non-democratic regimes on a world map, allowing users to inspect country-level regime histories and compare broad regional patterns. Another section focuses on coup d’état histories, showing successful and unsuccessful coup attempts for selected countries over time.
The modeling component uses survival analysis to study the time it takes for countries that transition away from democracy to return to it. The dashboard pairs model output with explanatory text so users can understand both the statistical approach and its interpretation.

title: Democracy Dashboard
text:
Deliverables

Live Shiny Dashboard
A deployed interactive dashboard for exploring global patterns in democracy, dictatorship, non-democratic spells, and coup d’état histories.
Launch Dashboard


GitHub Repository
Source code and project materials for the collaborative dashboard build.
View Repository

title: Profile
text:
About
I work at the intersection of statistics, AI, and organizational strategy. With a background in statistical consulting and collaboration, I use data to help teams define and navigate their toughest problems. My natural curiosity allows me to learn new domains quickly and implement intentional solutions that are grounded in the client’s mission.
Maximizing impact and engaging deeply with clients is the central topic of my research. Learn more about the project here.

title: Student Advocacy at Cal Poly
text:
Executive Summary
Associated Students, Inc. (ASI) is Cal Poly’s student-governed nonprofit auxiliary and the official voice of the student body. As an elected student representative on the ASI Board of Directors, I authored and co-authored legislation addressing institutional growth, shared governance, student representation, academic policy, and organizational structure.
This page summarizes selected resolutions I helped advance through ASI. Together, these items reflect my approach to advocacy: translating student concerns into formal policy language, building coalitions with student and institutional partners, and using governance processes to shape decisions that affect the student experience.

title: Student Advocacy at Cal Poly
text:
Advocacy Highlights

Authored and co-authored formal student government legislation on academic policy, institutional decision-making, and governance reform.
Advanced student perspectives through resolutions directed toward university administrators, Academic Senate processes, and ASI corporate governance.
Used policy writing, stakeholder communication, and board process to turn student concerns into actionable recommendations.

title: Student Advocacy at Cal Poly
text:
Methods & Approach
My legislative work began with identifying issues where student perspectives needed clearer representation in formal decision-making. From there, I worked with co-authors, student leaders, faculty, staff, and administrators to understand the relevant governance context, draft policy language, and build support before board consideration.
This work required balancing advocacy with institutional fluency. Strong resolutions needed to be principled enough to communicate student priorities, specific enough to guide action, and collaborative enough to move through shared governance structures productively.

title: dictatR

title: dictatR
text:
Executive Summary
dictatR is an R package project designed to make global democracy and dictatorship data easier to explore, visualize, and analyze. The package provides tools for working with regime-type data in a more accessible workflow, helping users move from raw political data toward interpretable summaries and visualizations.
This project reflects my interest in building reusable tools that make statistical and data science workflows more efficient. Rather than creating a one-off analysis, dictatR packages common tasks into functions that can support future projects, teaching examples, and exploratory political data analysis.

title: dictatR
text:
Project Highlights

Developed an R package structure for working with global democracy and dictatorship data.
Created tools to support exploratory analysis, visualization, and interpretation of regime-type patterns.
Practiced package-oriented development, documentation, and reusable workflow design in R.

title: dictatR
text:
Methods & Approach
The project was built as an R package, which required organizing code into reusable functions, documenting expected inputs and outputs, and designing tools that can be applied across multiple analyses. The package focuses on making political regime data easier to manipulate and visualize, particularly for users interested in comparing democratic and non-democratic patterns across countries.
The broader goal was to move beyond a single analysis script and toward a more general toolkit. This made the project a useful exercise in software design, reproducible research, and statistical programming for social science data.

title: dictatR
text:
Deliverables

GitHub Repository
An R package project containing tools for exploring, visualizing, and analyzing global democracy and dictatorship data.

title: Statistical Consulting
text:

title: Statistical Consulting
text:
Executive Summary
This page summarizes a collection of statistical consulting projects across environmental science, health research, human factors, sustainability literacy, and student experience. Because many engagements involved confidential client data or unpublished student research, I have grouped them into a single overview rather than presenting each project as a standalone case study.
Across these projects, my role has been to help teams move from broad research questions to clear analytic strategies. That work often includes refining study goals, identifying appropriate statistical methods, conducting analysis, explaining tradeoffs, and translating results into language that decision-makers and domain experts can use.

title: Statistical Consulting
text:
Consulting Highlights

Supported applied research teams across environmental, biological, health, engineering, and other disciplines.
Helped translate complex substantive questions into statistical models, study designs, and interpretable findings.
Communicated results through written reports, cover memos, presentations, and client-ready recommendations.

title: Statistical Consulting
text:
Selected Engagements









Year
Project
Context
Methods




2026
Soil organic carbon relationships in vineyard soils
Evaluated whether relationships between soil organic carbon, mineral-associated organic carbon, and particulate organic carbon aligned with carbon saturation theory.
Linear, logarithmic, and exponential pattern assessment


2026
Microhabitat conditions and lizard physiology
Assessed how environmental conditions differed across microhabitats and campus locations using repeated observations.
Mixed-effects modeling, repeated-measures analysis


2026
Biofilm formation and bacterial cell density
Evaluated organism, surface material, and time effects on bacterial concentration and biofilm formation.
Mixed-effects modeling, repeated-measures analysis, pairwise comparisons


2026
Soil health and regenerative land management
Compared soil health indicators and land management practices against viticulturist-perceived outcomes.
Multiple correspondence analysis, ordinal regression, logistic regression


2025
Ergonomics task performance
Evaluated how hand dominance, wrist direction, and distraction affected time to unscrew a bolt in a confined mechanical task.
ANOVA, power analysis, blocked experimental design


2025
Postpartum technology use and maternal health
Investigated associations between mothers’ technology use, parenting stress, and depression during the early postpartum period.
Multiple logistic regression, simple linear regression


2025
Student sustainability literacy
Studied sustainability knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors across colleges and geographic regions.
Multivariate multiple linear regression, spatial data analysis


2024
Student employment experience
Led survey and analysis work to understand student employment experiences and inform program design.
Probability sampling, CAPI administration, project management

title: Statistical Consulting
text:
Methods & Approach
My consulting approach emphasizes problem definition before model selection. I work with collaborators to clarify the research question, understand the structure and limitations of the data, choose methods that match the design, and communicate results in a way that supports practical interpretation.
These projects required a mix of statistical modeling, study design, survey administration, data cleaning, assumption checking, and technical communication. Just as importantly, they required learning each client’s domain quickly enough to make statistical recommendations that were both methodologically sound and contextually useful.

title: Green Roofs and Environmental Performance

title: Green Roofs and Environmental Performance
text:
Executive Summary
This project investigated whether green roofs can reduce building-level heat exposure and improve environmental performance in urban settings. Using temperature and building data, I modeled the relationship between roof type and thermal outcomes to evaluate whether green roof infrastructure was associated with measurable cooling benefits.
The project connects applied statistical modeling with a practical sustainability question: how cities can use design interventions to reduce heat, improve climate resilience, and support more evidence-based urban planning decisions.

title: Green Roofs and Environmental Performance
text:
Project Highlights

Used regression modeling to estimate the relationship between green roof infrastructure and building temperature outcomes.
Translated an environmental performance question into a statistical analysis suitable for a research poster format.
Communicated technical results visually for an audience interested in sustainability, climate adaptation, and applied data analysis.

title: Green Roofs and Environmental Performance
text:
Methods & Approach
The analysis focused on comparing temperature outcomes across buildings with different roofing characteristics. I used regression-based methods to evaluate whether green roofs were associated with lower temperatures while accounting for other relevant building or environmental factors.
The poster emphasized both statistical interpretation and applied relevance, presenting model results in a way that connected environmental data to broader questions about urban heat mitigation and sustainable infrastructure.

title: Green Roofs and Environmental Performance
text:
Deliverables

Research Poster
A poster presentation summarizing the research question, modeling approach, key findings, and sustainability implications of the green roofs analysis.

title: Advanced R Computing Repository

The coursework focused on moving beyond script-based analysis toward more structured, reusable, and maintainable R workflows. Topics included Quarto documents and dashboards, Git-based version control, functional programming with purrr, JSON data, WebR integration, function writing, R package development, code testing, debugging, and speed optimization.
Across assignments and examples, the emphasis was on building complete end-to-end analyses that are reproducible, well-organized, and easier to extend.

Executive Summary
This course archive reflects foundational work in data science, with emphasis on acquiring, processing, visualizing, and modeling different types of data. The course introduced a full applied data science workflow, moving from exploratory summaries and visualization to machine learning, model evaluation, and ethical considerations.
Because the repository contains course materials that may not be appropriate to post publicly, the full archive is available upon request. This page summarizes the main competencies covered rather than presenting the materials directly.

title: Intro to Data Science Repository
text:
Project Highlights

Worked with tabular, textual, hierarchical, and geospatial data across applied data science exercises.
Built visualizations and summaries to uncover patterns, compare observations, and communicate findings.
Applied machine learning methods to real-world prediction problems, including classification, KNN, model tuning, and model evaluation.

title: Intro to Data Science Repository
text:
Methods & Approach
The coursework emphasized the full data science pipeline: acquiring and combining data, processing different data structures, exploring relationships through visualization, and applying predictive modeling methods. Topics included tabular summaries, multivariable visualization, distance-based methods, text analysis with TF-IDF, classification, unsupervised learning, hierarchical data, and data ethics.
The course culminated in a project proposal and poster presentation, connecting technical methods to a complete data science communication workflow.

title: Political Polling Teaching Article
text:

View Article

title: Political Polling Teaching Article
text:
Executive Summary
Taking the Next Step in Exploring the Literary Digest 1936 Poll is a peer-reviewed teaching article published in the Journal of Statistics and Data Science Education. The article revisits the famous 1936 Literary Digest poll, which is often used to introduce sampling bias, and develops additional classroom activities around data acquisition, cleaning, validation, visualization, and adjustment methods.
I contributed to the data preparation, review, and early drafting process. My work supported the article’s broader goal: helping instructors turn a familiar historical polling example into a richer learning opportunity for statistics and data science students.

title: Political Polling Teaching Article
text:
Project Highlights

Contributed to a peer-reviewed statistics education article focused on sampling bias, data validation, and polling adjustment.
Supported data cleaning and review of historical polling data from the 1924–1936 Literary Digest presidential election polls.
Helped develop instructional materials that invite students to work with imperfect data, evaluate bias, and compare adjustment strategies.

title: Political Polling Teaching Article
text:
Methods & Approach
The article uses the Literary Digest polling data as a historical case study for teaching statistical thinking. Beyond identifying the poll’s well-known sampling and nonresponse bias, the paper explores how students can work with original data sources, identify data quality issues, visualize prediction errors, and evaluate simple adjustment methods.
My contributions centered on supporting the data workflow and manuscript development. This included cleaning and checking historical data, reviewing analysis materials, and contributing to early drafts of the paper.

title: Political Polling Teaching Article
text:
Deliverables

Peer-Reviewed Paper
A published statistics education article that provides instructors with classroom activities and supporting materials for teaching data cleaning, sampling bias, and polling adjustment through the 1936 Literary Digest poll.

title: Complex Instruction and Pair Programming
text:
Collaborative research paper examining how structured pair programming can support more equitable participation in introductory data science courses.

View Paper

title: Complex Instruction and Pair Programming
text:
Collaborative research paper examining how structured pair programming can support more equitable participation in introductory data science courses.

View Paper

title: Complex Instruction and Pair Programming
text:
Executive Summary
Status, Equity, and Collaboration is a collaborative research paper from a multi-institutional project studying how pair programming can be structured to support more equitable participation in introductory data science courses. The study examines student interactions at Cal Poly and CSU Monterey Bay, focusing on how power, authority, and status shape collaboration during programming activities.
The project adapts principles from Complex Instruction, a pedagogical framework designed to address unequal participation in group work. Rather than assuming pair programming is automatically equitable, the study investigates how role design, classroom status dynamics, and student identities influence who contributes, whose ideas are taken up, and how collaborative learning can be improved.

title: Complex Instruction and Pair Programming
text:
Project Highlights

Contributed to a collaborative pilot study on equity, status, and participation in introductory data science classrooms.
Helped examine how modified pair programming roles shaped student collaboration across two institutional contexts.
Supported research connecting data science education, discourse analysis, and equity-focused instructional design.

title: Complex Instruction and Pair Programming
text:
Methods & Approach
The study analyzed recorded and transcribed pair-programming interactions from introductory data science courses at Cal Poly and CSU Monterey Bay. The research team examined student contributions using discourse-analytic methods, focusing on proposal negotiation units and measures of intellectual merit.
The intervention replaced the traditional driver-navigator model with revised collaborative roles designed to separate strategic decision-making from keyboard control. The study then evaluated whether students recognized and followed those roles, how role talk appeared during collaboration, and whether the protocol reduced differences in intellectual influence between partners.
Findings from the pilot study informed revisions to the collaborative protocol, including clearer role names, more explicit responsibilities, and tangible role structures intended to reduce the influence of status characteristics such as gender, major, and prior programming experience.

title: Complex Instruction and Pair Programming
text:
Deliverables

Proceedings Paper
A collaborative paper summarizing the pilot study, pair programming intervention, discourse-analysis framework, key findings, and protocol revisions for supporting more equitable collaboration in data science education.
View Paper

title: Complex Instruction and Pair Programming
text:
Skills

Discourse Analysis Equity-Focused Research Data Science Education Research Communication

title: Data Viz for Nonprofits

Workshop materials helping nonprofit teams collect, explore, and communicate data in ways that elevate community impact.

View Slides

title: Data Viz for Nonprofits
text:
Workshop materials helping nonprofit teams collect, explore, and communicate data in ways that elevate community impact.

View Slides

title: Data Viz for Nonprofits
text:
Executive Summary
This workshop was designed to help nonprofit organizations use data more intentionally in support of their missions. The session focused on practical steps for designing data collection around organizational goals, collecting higher-quality information, and turning raw data into clear visual summaries that support decision-making.
Rather than treating data visualization as a purely technical exercise, the workshop emphasized purpose, audience, and action. Participants were encouraged to begin by asking what they want to learn, who needs the information, and how the results could support planning, communication, or community impact.

title: Data Viz for Nonprofits
text:
Project Highlights

Co-developed workshop materials that introduced nonprofit teams to a practical data utilization workflow.
Helped participants connect data collection decisions to organizational purpose, audience, and mission-aligned questions.
Demonstrated how simple visual summaries can turn raw data into clearer insights for planning, reporting, and communication.

title: Data Viz for Nonprofits
text:
Methods & Approach
The workshop followed a four-part structure: design, collect, explore, and discussion. The design section helped participants clarify their purpose, audience, and guiding questions. The collection section focused on practical strategies for improving data quality, including choosing appropriate collection methods, using organized digital tools, establishing regular collection processes, and reducing inconsistencies in stored data.
The exploration section introduced a simple workflow for moving from raw information to usable insight: familiarize yourself with the data, choose what to investigate, visualize findings, and communicate results clearly to the intended audience. A sample meal-service scenario showed how an organization could collect daily service counts, organize them in a spreadsheet, and visualize trends over time.

title: Data Viz for Nonprofits
text:
Deliverables

Workshop Slides
A slide deck introducing nonprofit teams to data collection, exploration, and visualization practices that support clearer communication and stronger community impact.
View Slides

title: Data Viz for Nonprofits
text:
Skills

Data Visualization Workshop Design Nonprofit Support Data Collection Community Engagement
